title: Dashrath Rupsingh Rathod v. State of Maharashtra
bench: T. S. Thakur, Vikramajit Sen, C. Nagappan, JJ.
bns_mapping: NI Act S. 138 — unchanged (BNS does not affect special laws)
CrPC S. 177 → BNSS, 2023 S. 197 (territorial jurisdiction — provision substantially same)
facts: Complaint under S. 138 of NI Act filed at place where complainant presented cheque for encashment, not where drawee bank dishonoured it. The question was whether the place of presentation by the payee or the place of dishonour by the drawee bank determines territorial jurisdiction.
issues: Whether territorial jurisdiction for an offence under S. 138 NI Act lies only at the place where the drawee bank dishonours the cheque, or also at the places identified in K. Bhaskaran (1999)?
holding: The offence under S. 138 is constituted only when the drawee bank dishonours the cheque on presentment. The place where such dishonour occurs is the sole situs of offence; only the Court within whose territorial jurisdiction the drawee branch is located can take cognizance. Place of issuance of cheque, place where complainant presents to collecting bank, and place of issue or delivery of statutory notice under proviso (b) are not relevant to territorial jurisdiction. K. Bhaskaran v. Sankaran Vaidhyan Balan (1999) 7 SCC 510 to that extent overruled.
subsequent_treatment: Effect partly nullified by Negotiable Instruments (Amendment) Act, 2015 (w.e.f. 15-6-2015), which inserted Sections 142(2) and 142A — jurisdiction now lies where the payee maintains the account into which the cheque was deposited. But the case remains foundational for pre-2015 cheques and the jurisprudential reasoning.

title: K. Bhaskaran v. Sankaran Vaidhyan Balan
bench: K. T. Thomas, M. Srinivasan, JJ.
facts: Foundational case interpreting the components of the offence under S. 138.
issues: What constitutes the offence under S. 138 NI Act?
What is the appropriate court for trial?
holding: The offence under S. 138 has five components: (1) drawing of cheque, (2) presentation to bank, (3) returning unpaid by drawee bank, (4) giving notice in writing to drawer demanding payment, (5) failure of drawer to pay within 15 days of receipt of notice. Each constitutes part of cause of action; complaint can be filed at any place where any of these five components occur.
subsequent_treatment: The 'five components' jurisdictional rule overruled in Dashrath Rupsingh Rathod v. State of Maharashtra (2014) 9 SCC 129; restored in modified form by NI (Amendment) Act, 2015. The 'five components' analysis of the offence itself is still good law.

title: Modi Cements Ltd. v. Kuchil Kumar Nandi
bench: M. K. Mukherjee, S. Saghir Ahmad, JJ.
facts: Drawer issued a 'stop payment' instruction to the bank, leading to dishonour.
issues: Whether dishonour due to 'stop payment' instructions falls within S. 138 NI Act?
holding: Dishonour of cheque on the ground that drawer had instructed the bank to 'stop payment' attracts S. 138, just as dishonour for insufficiency of funds does. The expression 'amount of money standing to the credit of that account is insufficient' must be construed broadly to include all situations where the cheque is returned unpaid. Earlier contrary view in Electronics Trade & Technology Development Corpn. v. Indian Technologists & Engineers (Electronics) (P) Ltd. (1996) 2 SCC 739 distinguished.
subsequent_treatment: Followed and reaffirmed in numerous subsequent decisions; still good law.

title: C.C. Alavi Haji v. Palapetty Muhammed
bench: K. G. Balakrishnan, CJ., R. V. Raveendran, D. K. Jain, JJ.
statutes: Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881 — S. 138, S. 27 General Clauses Act, 1897
facts: Drawer claimed he never received the statutory notice under proviso (b) to S. 138.
issues: Whether service of notice can be presumed when notice is sent by registered post and returned with endorsement 'unclaimed' or 'refused'?
holding: When a notice is sent by registered post to the correct address and returned 'unclaimed' or 'refused', service is presumed under Section 27 of the General Clauses Act, 1897 read with Section 114 of the Evidence Act. The drawer can rebut this presumption by showing non-service was for reasons beyond his control. Mere denial of receipt is insufficient.
subsequent_treatment: Followed consistently. Forms the basis of standard practice in S. 138 prosecutions.

title: Suman Sethi v. Ajay K. Churiwal
bench: G. T. Nanavati, S. N. Phukan, JJ.
facts: Notice issued under proviso (b) to S. 138 demanded a sum larger than the cheque amount, including damages and interest.
issues: Is a notice under proviso (b) to S. 138 valid if it demands an amount in excess of the dishonoured cheque amount?
holding: A notice under S. 138 proviso (b) is valid even if it claims an amount in excess of the cheque amount, provided the demand for the cheque amount is identifiable separately or is the substantive demand. The statute requires demand for 'said amount of money' (i.e. cheque amount) — additional claims do not vitiate the notice if the cheque amount is clearly demanded.
subsequent_treatment: Followed and applied in numerous cases.

title: Gurbaksh Singh Sibbia v. State of Punjab
bench: Y. V. Chandrachud, CJ., P. N. Bhagwati, N. L. Untwalia, R. S. Pathak, O. Chinnappa Reddy, JJ.
bns_mapping: CrPC S. 438 → BNSS, 2023 S. 482 (anticipatory bail) — provision substantially retained
facts: Several Punjab state ministers and politicians apprehending arrest sought anticipatory bail. The Punjab and Haryana High Court had imposed restrictive conditions, holding anticipatory bail to be exceptional.
issues: What is the scope, ambit, and conditions for grant of anticipatory bail under S. 438 CrPC?
holding: S. 438 CrPC is a procedural provision concerned with personal liberty under Article 21 of the Constitution. It is not to be construed restrictively. Anticipatory bail is not 'extraordinary' relief but a salutary provision. Courts should not impose unwarranted restrictions; the discretion is to be exercised judicially based on facts of each case. There is no necessity that the apprehended accusation must be of a specific offence; reasonable apprehension of arrest in non-bailable case is sufficient. Duration of anticipatory bail is generally not limited unless the court considers it necessary.
subsequent_treatment: Foundational. Repeatedly affirmed; principles refined in Sushila Aggarwal v. State (NCT of Delhi) (2020) 5 SCC 1.

title: Sushila Aggarwal v. State (NCT of Delhi)
bench: Arun Mishra, Indira Banerjee, Vineet Saran, M. R. Shah, S. Ravindra Bhat, JJ.
facts: Reference to Constitution Bench on the question of duration of anticipatory bail.
issues: Whether anticipatory bail granted under S. 438 CrPC should be limited in time?
Whether protection ends on the accused being summoned by the court, or on appearance, or until conclusion of trial?
holding: Grant of anticipatory bail should not invariably be limited in time; ordinarily, no time limit need be fixed. The protection continues until the conclusion of trial. However, in special and peculiar circumstances, a court may impose limited duration. The mere filing of charge sheet, taking of cognizance, or summoning of the accused does not automatically terminate anticipatory bail. Restrictions can be imposed under S. 438(2) CrPC having regard to facts of the case.
subsequent_treatment: Reaffirms and clarifies Gurbaksh Singh Sibbia. Currently the leading authority.

title: State of Haryana v. Bhajan Lal
bench: S. R. Pandian, K. Jayachandra Reddy, JJ.
bns_mapping: CrPC S. 482 → BNSS, 2023 S. 528 (inherent powers of High Court)
facts: FIR registered against the then Chief Minister of Haryana on allegations of corruption. He sought quashing under S. 482 CrPC and Article 226.
issues: What are the parameters for quashing an FIR or criminal proceedings under S. 482 CrPC / Article 226?
holding: Seven illustrative categories where High Court may exercise inherent powers to quash FIR/criminal proceedings: (1) allegations in FIR even taken at face value do not constitute offence, (2) allegations do not disclose cognizable offence justifying investigation, (3) uncontroverted allegations and evidence collected do not disclose commission of any offence, (4) allegations only constitute non-cognizable offence (no permission of Magistrate under S. 155(2)), (5) allegations are absurd and inherently improbable, (6) express legal bar to institution of proceedings, (7) proceeding manifestly attended with mala fides or instituted maliciously with ulterior motive. Power must be exercised sparingly and with caution.
subsequent_treatment: The most-cited quashing precedent; routinely applied. Survives the BNSS transition.

title: Parbatbhai Aahir v. State of Gujarat
bench: Dipak Misra, A. M. Khanwilkar, D. Y. Chandrachud, JJ.
facts: Parties sought quashing of FIR involving offences under S. 307 IPC on the basis of compromise.
issues: Can High Court quash an FIR on the basis of compromise where serious offences (like S. 307 IPC) are involved?
holding: Settlement-based quashing is appropriate for civil-flavoured disputes (commercial, financial, matrimonial). However, heinous and serious offences such as those involving moral turpitude, murder, rape, dacoity, or offences against the State cannot be quashed merely on settlement, even if the parties have settled. For S. 307 IPC, the High Court should consider the gravity of injury, the nature of weapon used, the number of injuries, and other circumstances; compromise alone is not sufficient. Distinguishes Gian Singh v. State of Punjab (2012) 10 SCC 303 on degree of seriousness.
subsequent_treatment: Leading authority on settlement-based quashing of serious offences.

title: Virsa Singh v. State of Punjab
bench: Vivian Bose, S. Jafer Imam, A. K. Sarkar, JJ.
bns_mapping: IPC S. 300 → BNS, 2023 S. 101 (definition of murder — substantially retained)
topics: bodily injury sufficient to cause death
facts: Single spear thrust into the abdomen of the victim resulted in death.
issues: What are the ingredients required to bring a case under the third clause of S. 300 IPC (intention to inflict bodily injury sufficient in the ordinary course of nature to cause death)?
holding: Four ingredients must be proved under the third clause of S. 300 IPC: (1) bodily injury is present, (2) nature of the injury must be proved, (3) it must be proved that there was an intention to inflict that particular bodily injury (not just any injury), (4) it must be proved that the injury was sufficient in the ordinary course of nature to cause death. The fourth element is purely objective and inferential. Once the prosecution proves these four facts, the offence is murder under S. 300 IPC; whether the accused intended death or not is irrelevant.
subsequent_treatment: Foundational. Cited in virtually every murder case; principles continue to apply under BNS S. 101.

title: K.M. Nanavati v. State of Maharashtra
bench: K. Subba Rao, S. K. Das, Raghubar Dayal, JJ.
statutes: Penal Code, 1860 — S. 300, Exception 1 (grave and sudden provocation)
facts: Naval officer Nanavati shot dead his wife's lover after learning of the affair. Question of whether the killing was on grave and sudden provocation.
issues: What constitutes 'grave and sudden provocation' under Exception 1 to S. 300 IPC?
holding: The test of 'grave and sudden provocation' is whether a reasonable man, belonging to the same class of society as the accused, placed in the situation in which the accused was placed, would be so provoked as to lose his self-control. The provocation need not be only by acts of the deceased but also by words or acts of others. The fatal blow must be clearly traced to the influence of passion arising from that provocation and not after passion had cooled down (i.e. there must be no time for the passion to cool). The mental state of the accused, the gravity of the provocation, and the time gap between provocation and act are all relevant.
subsequent_treatment: Foundational. The 'reasonable man' test continues to apply under BNS Exception 1 to S. 101.

title: Tukaram v. State of Maharashtra (Mathura case)
bench: Jaswant Singh, P. S. Kailasam, A. D. Koshal, JJ.
facts: Mathura, a young tribal girl, was allegedly raped by two policemen at Desai Ganj police station. Sessions court acquitted; High Court convicted; Supreme Court reversed and acquitted.
issues: What constitutes consent in custodial settings? Burden of proof?
holding: (Historical) The Supreme Court held that absence of injuries on the prosecutrix and her not raising alarm indicated 'passive consent', not rape. The acquittal led to widespread public outcry and the Criminal Law (Amendment) Act, 1983 inserting S. 114-A Evidence Act (presumption of absence of consent in custodial rape) and S. 376(2) IPC creating aggravated forms.
subsequent_treatment: Effectively overruled by 1983 amendments; widely criticized. Historical reference only — current law on custodial rape is governed by BNS S. 64(2) and Section 120 BSA (presumption against consent).

title: Hridaya Ranjan Prasad Verma v. State of Bihar
bench: D. P. Wadhwa, M. Srinivasan, JJ.
facts: Complaint of cheating arising out of a contractual breach. The question was whether mere breach of promise constitutes cheating.
issues: When does breach of contract amount to cheating under S. 420 IPC?
holding: Mere breach of contract does not constitute cheating. To constitute cheating, the dishonest or fraudulent intention must exist at the time of making the promise or representation; subsequent failure to honour the promise does not by itself raise the inference of dishonest intention. The complaint must show that there was no intention to perform from the very beginning. Where dispute is essentially civil in nature, criminal proceedings would be an abuse of process.
subsequent_treatment: Foundational principle, repeatedly applied; basis for quashing of cheating cases involving civil disputes.

title: State of Punjab v. Baldev Singh
bench: S. C. Agrawal, G. T. Nanavati, S. P. Bharucha, S. Saghir Ahmad, K. Venkataswami, JJ.
statutes: Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances Act, 1985 — S. 50
bns_mapping: NDPS Act unaffected by BNS/BNSS transition (special law)
topics: right to be searched before Magistrate or Gazetted Officer
facts: Question of whether failure to comply with S. 50 NDPS Act (informing accused of right to be searched before a Gazetted Officer or Magistrate) vitiates the trial.
issues: Is the requirement under S. 50 NDPS Act mandatory? What is the consequence of non-compliance?
holding: (1) S. 50 is mandatory. (2) The accused must be informed of his right to be searched before a Gazetted Officer or Magistrate; mere asking 'do you want to be searched before a Magistrate' is insufficient. (3) Failure to comply with S. 50 affects the prosecution case to the extent of any evidence collected through the illegal search. (4) Such failure may not necessarily vitiate the entire trial if other independent evidence exists. Recovery itself becomes suspect; the conviction cannot be based on tainted recovery alone.
subsequent_treatment: Refined in Vijaysinh Chandubha Jadeja v. State of Gujarat (2011) 1 SCC 609 (Constitution Bench) — must inform of right in writing; further refined in Mohan Lal v. State of Punjab (2018) 17 SCC 627.

title: Vijaysinh Chandubha Jadeja v. State of Gujarat
bench: K. G. Balakrishnan, CJ., R. V. Raveendran, D. K. Jain, P. Sathasivam, J. M. Panchal, JJ.
statutes: Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances Act, 1985 — S. 50
facts: Reference to Constitution Bench on whether mere information about right under S. 50 is sufficient or whether the accused must be informed in writing.
issues: What is the precise nature of the obligation under S. 50 NDPS Act?
holding: The accused must be informed of his right under S. 50 in writing. The communication must be unambiguous; merely asking whether the accused wants to be searched before a Magistrate or Gazetted Officer is not enough. The accused must be told that he has the right to be searched before a Magistrate or Gazetted Officer if he so desires. This is a mandatory protective requirement and any non-compliance vitiates the search and renders the recovery inadmissible to that extent.
subsequent_treatment: Reaffirmed in Mohan Lal v. State of Punjab (2018) 17 SCC 627.

title: Lalita Kumari v. Govt of U.P.
bench: P. Sathasivam, CJ., B. S. Chauhan, Ranjana Prakash Desai, Ranjan Gogoi, S. A. Bobde, JJ.
bns_mapping: CrPC S. 154 → BNSS, 2023 S. 173 (information to police — substantially retained, with addition of e-FIR)
facts: Whether police are obligated to register an FIR upon receiving information disclosing a cognizable offence, or whether they may conduct a preliminary inquiry first.
issues: Is registration of FIR under S. 154 CrPC mandatory upon information disclosing a cognizable offence?
When may a preliminary inquiry be conducted?
holding: Registration of FIR is mandatory if the information discloses commission of a cognizable offence; no preliminary inquiry is permissible in such situations. Action must be taken against erring officers who do not register FIR. A preliminary inquiry may be conducted only where it is required to ascertain whether the information reveals a commission of a cognizable offence in specific categories: matrimonial/family disputes, commercial offences, medical negligence, corruption cases, and cases of abnormal delay (>3 months) in initiating prosecution. The scope is limited to ascertaining whether a cognizable offence is disclosed; not to verify the truth of the allegation.
subsequent_treatment: Foundational. Survives BNSS transition; principles apply to S. 173 BNSS.

title: Arnesh Kumar v. State of Bihar
bench: C. K. Prasad, Pinaki Chandra Ghose, JJ.
statutes: Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — S. 41, S. 41-A
facts: Anticipatory bail in a matrimonial dispute under S. 498-A IPC. The Court took the opportunity to issue directions on automatic arrest in such cases.
issues: What guidelines should govern arrest in offences punishable up to 7 years?
holding: Police officers shall not automatically arrest in cases under S. 498-A IPC and other offences punishable up to 7 years. Before arrest, the officer must satisfy himself about the necessity of arrest under the parameters in S. 41(1)(b)(ii) CrPC (now S. 35 BNSS). A checklist must be filled and forwarded to the Magistrate. In other cases, notice of appearance under S. 41-A CrPC (now S. 35(3) BNSS) must be issued. Failure to comply with these directions makes the officer liable for departmental action and the Magistrate liable for action by the High Court. Authorising detention by the Magistrate without recording satisfaction is also impermissible.
subsequent_treatment: Survives BNSS transition; directions apply under new procedural code.

title: Joginder Kumar v. State of U.P.
bench: M. N. Venkatachaliah, CJ., S. Mohan, A. S. Anand, JJ.
statutes: Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — S. 41, S. 50
facts: Petitioner was arrested by U.P. police; relatives were not informed.
issues: What rights does an arrested person have? What duties do the police have at the time of arrest?
holding: Arrest cannot be made merely on suspicion without satisfaction of necessity. The arresting officer must have justifiable reasons recorded in writing. The arrested person has the right to: (1) inform a friend/relative of arrest, (2) be informed of this right, (3) be produced before a Magistrate within 24 hours, (4) consult a lawyer of his choice. Police are required to make an entry in the diary regarding arrest and persons informed.
subsequent_treatment: Built upon in D.K. Basu v. State of W.B. (1997) 1 SCC 416. Survives transition; applies under BNSS.

title: D.K. Basu v. State of West Bengal
bench: Kuldip Singh, A. S. Anand, JJ.
bns_mapping: Principles incorporated in BNSS provisions on arrest (Ss. 35, 47)
facts: Letter petition treated as PIL; concerned with custodial deaths and torture.
issues: What guidelines must police follow to prevent custodial torture and death?
holding: Eleven detailed requirements: (1) police personnel making arrest must wear accurate, visible name tags with designations, (2) memo of arrest prepared at time of arrest, attested by family member or respectable person, countersigned by arrestee with date and time, (3) friend or relative to be informed of arrest with place of detention as soon as possible, (4) when next friend lives outside, arrestee’s rights and place of detention to be communicated to legal aid organisation, (5) arrestee must be told of his right to have someone informed, (6) entry in arrest register, (7) arrestee may, if requested, be examined at time of arrest with major and minor injuries recorded, (8) medical examination every 48 hours during detention by qualified doctor, (9) copies of all arrest documents to Magistrate, (10) arrestee permitted to meet lawyer during interrogation though not throughout, (11) police control room at every district/state HQ to display arrests.
subsequent_treatment: Foundational. Codified in subsequent CrPC amendments (2009) and now in BNSS.

title: Sharad Birdhichand Sarda v. State of Maharashtra
bench: Y. V. Chandrachud, CJ., S. Murtaza Fazal Ali, A. Varadarajan, JJ.
bns_mapping: Evidence Act → Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 — S. 2 (definitions); principle continues
facts: Murder case based entirely on circumstantial evidence — death of newly-married wife by potassium cyanide poisoning.
issues: What are the principles governing conviction based on circumstantial evidence?
holding: Five 'golden principles' (panchsheel) for conviction based on circumstantial evidence: (1) circumstances from which the conclusion of guilt is to be drawn must be fully established, (2) the facts so established must be consistent only with the hypothesis of guilt, (3) circumstances must be of conclusive nature and tendency, (4) they must exclude every possible hypothesis except the one to be proved, (5) there must be a chain of evidence so complete as not to leave any reasonable ground for the conclusion consistent with innocence and must show that in all human probability the act must have been done by the accused.
subsequent_treatment: The most-cited authority on circumstantial evidence in Indian criminal law. Survives the BSA transition.

title: Vijay Madanlal Choudhary v. Union of India
bench: A. M. Khanwilkar, Dinesh Maheshwari, C. T. Ravikumar, JJ.
statutes: Prevention of Money Laundering Act, 2002 — S. 3, S. 5, S. 17, S. 19, S. 24, S. 45, S. 50
facts: Constitutional challenge to several provisions of PMLA — powers of arrest, search, seizure, attachment; reverse burden of proof; twin conditions for bail.
issues: Constitutional validity of multiple PMLA provisions including S. 19 (arrest), S. 24 (presumption), S. 45 (bail conditions), S. 50 (statements).
holding: (1) PMLA provisions upheld. (2) ECIR is not equivalent to FIR; supply of ECIR not mandatory. (3) Statements under S. 50 PMLA before ED officials are not hit by Article 20(3) — ED officials are not 'police officers'. (4) Twin conditions in S. 45 (release on bail only if Court has reasonable grounds for believing accused not guilty and not likely to commit offence while on bail) are constitutional. (5) Reverse burden under S. 24 valid. (6) Power of attachment under S. 5 valid.
subsequent_treatment: Subject to review (pending). Refined by Pankaj Bansal v. UOI (2023) — grounds of arrest must be furnished in writing.

title: Pankaj Bansal v. Union of India
bench: A. S. Bopanna, Sanjay Kumar, JJ.
statutes: Prevention of Money Laundering Act, 2002 — S. 19
facts: Petitioners arrested under PMLA. Grounds of arrest were not furnished in writing.
issues: Whether grounds of arrest under S. 19 PMLA must be communicated in writing?
holding: Grounds of arrest under S. 19(1) PMLA must be furnished to the arrestee in writing as a matter of course and without exception. Mere oral communication is insufficient. This is a fundamental requirement under Article 22(1) of the Constitution. Failure to furnish written grounds renders the arrest illegal and the arrestee entitled to release.
subsequent_treatment: Foundational; extended to UAPA arrests in Prabir Purkayastha v. State (NCT of Delhi) (2024) 8 SCC 254.

title: National Investigation Agency v. Zahoor Ahmad Shah Watali
bench: A. M. Khanwilkar, Ajay Rastogi, JJ.
facts: NIA appealed against grant of bail to UAPA accused.
issues: What is the standard for grant of bail under S. 43-D(5) of UAPA?
holding: While considering bail under S. 43-D(5) UAPA, the Court must examine whether there are reasonable grounds for believing that the accusation against the person is prima facie true. The Court must take the case diary or final report into consideration. The accused will not be released on bail if the Court is of opinion that there are reasonable grounds for believing the accusations are prima facie true. The standard is much higher than ordinary bail jurisprudence — the Court is to take material at face value without analysing weight or admissibility in detail.
subsequent_treatment: Modified in Union of India v. K.A. Najeeb (2021) 3 SCC 713 — long pretrial incarceration may itself be a ground to grant bail despite Watali.

title: Union of India v. K.A. Najeeb
bench: N. V. Ramana, Surya Kant, Aniruddha Bose, JJ.
facts: Accused under UAPA had been in pretrial detention for over 5 years; trial unlikely to conclude soon.
issues: Whether constitutional courts can grant bail under UAPA on the ground of long incarceration despite the rigours of S. 43-D(5)?
holding: Statutory restrictions like S. 43-D(5) UAPA do not oust the constitutional courts' power to grant bail on the ground of violation of Part III rights, including Article 21. Where trial has been delayed and the accused has undergone substantial period of incarceration, the courts are not powerless to grant bail despite Watali. The longer the incarceration without trial, the stronger the case for bail. The constitutional courts must balance statutory rigour with Article 21.
subsequent_treatment: Reaffirmed in Vernon v. State of Maharashtra (2023) and Sheikh Javed Iqbal v. State of UP (2024). Major check on UAPA detentions.

title: Sanjay Chandra v. CBI
bench: G. S. Singhvi, H. L. Dattu, JJ.
statutes: Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — S. 437, S. 439
facts: 2G spectrum scam accused sought bail. CBI opposed citing gravity of economic offences.
issues: Is gravity of allegations alone sufficient to deny bail in economic offences?
holding: The basic rule is bail, not jail, except where there are circumstances suggestive of fleeing from justice or thwarting the course of justice or creating other troubles. The seriousness of the charges may be one factor but cannot be the sole consideration. Pretrial detention is punitive in nature and offends the presumption of innocence. The principle 'bail is the rule, jail is the exception' applies to economic offences as well. Courts must examine: nature of accusations, nature of evidence, severity of punishment, character of accused, possibility of accused fleeing or tampering with evidence.
subsequent_treatment: Routinely cited in economic offence bail matters; survives BNSS transition.

title: State of Punjab v. Gurmit Singh
bench: Dr. A. S. Anand, S. Saghir Ahmad, JJ.
facts: Acquittal in rape case despite victim's testimony; reversed by Supreme Court.
issues: What is the evidentiary value of the testimony of a rape victim?
holding: Testimony of a rape victim is on a higher pedestal than that of an injured witness. If found credible, it does not require corroboration; corroboration may be sought as a matter of caution and not as a rule. The court must approach the evidence with sensitivity; minor discrepancies, delay in lodging FIR, or absence of injuries should not lead to wholesale rejection. Trial in rape cases must be conducted in camera; identity of victim must be protected.
subsequent_treatment: Foundational. Principles continue under BNS S. 64 and BSA S. 120.

title: Independent Thought v. Union of India
bench: Madan B. Lokur, Deepak Gupta, JJ.
statutes: Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012 — S. 3
facts: Challenge to Exception 2 to S. 375 IPC which provided that sexual intercourse by a man with his wife not under 15 years was not rape.
issues: Whether Exception 2 to S. 375 IPC, insofar as it allows sexual intercourse with a wife between 15-18 years, is constitutional?
holding: Exception 2 to S. 375 IPC is unconstitutional insofar as it permits sexual intercourse with a girl child wife between 15 and 18 years. Such intercourse constitutes rape. The Exception is read down to apply only to wives 18 years or older. The Court did not address the broader question of marital rape of adult wives.
subsequent_treatment: Followed and applied; the broader marital rape question pending in RIT Foundation v. UOI (referred to larger bench in 2022).

title: Kedar Nath Singh v. State of Bihar
bench: B. P. Sinha, CJ., S. K. Das, A. K. Sarkar, N. Rajagopala Ayyangar, J. R. Mudholkar, JJ.
bns_mapping: IPC S. 124-A removed; replaced by BNS S. 152 (acts endangering sovereignty, unity and integrity of India). Provision substantially restructured.
facts: Speech by communist leader leading to prosecution under S. 124-A IPC.
issues: Constitutional validity of S. 124-A IPC; what is the scope of sedition?
holding: S. 124-A IPC is constitutionally valid but must be read down to apply only to acts involving incitement to violence or which create public disorder; mere strong criticism of the government is not seditious. The expression 'disaffection' must be read as comprehending only those activities that have the tendency to create public disorder. Protected speech under Article 19(1)(a) cannot be criminalised.
subsequent_treatment: Effect of judgment continues. S. 124-A IPC was kept in abeyance by SC in S.G. Vombatkere v. UOI (2022). Replaced by BNS S. 152, which is also subject to challenge as it does not strictly apply Kedar Nath limitations.

title: Siddharam Satlingappa Mhetre v. State of Maharashtra
bench: Dalveer Bhandari, K. S. Panicker Radhakrishnan, JJ.
facts: High Court denied anticipatory bail relying on restrictive interpretations.
issues: What considerations should govern grant of anticipatory bail under S. 438 CrPC?
holding: Detailed factors for grant of anticipatory bail: nature and gravity of accusation; antecedents of applicant; possibility of applicant fleeing from justice; possibility of repeat offence; reasonable apprehension of evidence tampering; intent of applicant to evade trial; manner of accusation; impact on victim; larger public interest. Anticipatory bail is a statutory right and should not be refused merely because the applicant is alleged in a serious offence; consider all factors holistically. Imposition of mechanical conditions discouraged.
subsequent_treatment: Refined and consolidated by Sushila Aggarwal v. State (NCT of Delhi) (2020) 5 SCC 1.

title: Mohan Lal v. State of Punjab
bench: Ranjan Gogoi, R. Banumathi, Navin Sinha, JJ.
statutes: Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances Act, 1985 — S. 50
facts: Same officer was both complainant (informant) and investigating officer in NDPS case.
issues: Whether the same officer can act as informant/complainant and also as the investigating officer in NDPS cases?
holding: A fair investigation requires that the officer who lodges the complaint or is the informant should not be the same officer who investigates the case; this principle is integral to a fair trial under Article 21 in NDPS prosecutions. Where the same officer plays both roles, the investigation is vitiated and the conviction cannot stand.
subsequent_treatment: Reaffirmed in Mukesh Singh v. State (Narcotic Branch of Delhi) (2020) 10 SCC 120 — Constitution Bench partially clarified the rule.

title: Mukesh Singh v. State (Narcotic Branch of Delhi)
bench: Arun Mishra, Indira Banerjee, Vineet Saran, M. R. Shah, S. Ravindra Bhat, JJ.
statutes: Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances Act, 1985 — S. 50, S. 53
facts: Reference on whether the rule in Mohan Lal v. State of Punjab applies as an absolute bar.
issues: Does the same officer being informant and investigator vitiate trial in every case?
holding: The question whether the trial is vitiated depends on the facts of each case. There is no absolute bar on the same officer acting as informant and investigator. Such circumstance is one factor in evaluating fairness; fair investigation must be judged on totality of evidence. Mohan Lal does not lay down an inflexible rule.
subsequent_treatment: Currently the leading authority; partially modifies Mohan Lal.

title: Gian Singh v. State of Punjab
bench: R. M. Lodha, A. R. Dave, S. J. Mukhopadhaya, JJ.
statutes: Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — S. 320, S. 482
facts: Reference on whether non-compoundable offences can be quashed on basis of compromise.
issues: What is the scope of S. 482 CrPC for quashing non-compoundable offences on basis of settlement?
holding: S. 482 CrPC is independent of S. 320 CrPC. The High Court has inherent power to quash non-compoundable offences on settlement, but this power must be exercised carefully. Heinous offences, offences against society at large, or those affecting public interest cannot be quashed even with compromise. Civil-flavoured disputes (commercial, matrimonial, family) can be quashed. Each case to be examined on its own facts.
subsequent_treatment: Refined in Parbatbhai Aahir v. State of Gujarat (2017) 9 SCC 641 (heinous offences cannot be quashed even on settlement).

title: State of Maharashtra v. Maroti
bench: M. R. Shah, C. T. Ravikumar, JJ.
statutes: Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012 — S. 4, S. 6
facts: POCSO conviction; defence challenged the determination of victim's age.
issues: What is the proper method of age determination of a minor victim under POCSO Act?
holding: Age determination must follow the hierarchy in the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Rules, 2007/2016: (1) date of birth from school certificate, (2) matriculation certificate, (3) birth certificate from corporation/municipality, (4) medical age determination by ossification test as a last resort. Ossification test has wide margin of error and should be used cautiously. The benefit of doubt in age determination should generally go to the victim only insofar as necessary for justice.
subsequent_treatment: Followed in subsequent POCSO matters.

title: Prabir Purkayastha v. State (NCT of Delhi)
bench: B. R. Gavai, Sandeep Mehta, JJ.
facts: Editor of NewsClick arrested under UAPA without written grounds furnished.
issues: Does the Pankaj Bansal requirement of written grounds of arrest apply to UAPA arrests?
holding: The constitutional requirement under Article 22(1) that grounds of arrest must be communicated in writing applies to all statutes including UAPA, not just PMLA. The principle in Pankaj Bansal v. UOI (2023) extends to UAPA. Failure to furnish written grounds vitiates the arrest and the arrestee is entitled to release, regardless of the seriousness of the offence.
subsequent_treatment: Strong precedent; extended in Vihaan Kumar v. State of Haryana (2025) to all arrests.

title: Satya Pal Singh v. State of Madhya Pradesh
bench: V. Gopala Gowda, R. Banumathi, JJ.
facts: Victim's father sought to appeal acquittal under proviso to S. 372 CrPC.
issues: Who is a 'victim' for purposes of proviso to S. 372 CrPC and entitled to file appeal against acquittal?
holding: The proviso to S. 372 CrPC (inserted in 2009) gives a victim the right to appeal against any order acquitting the accused, convicting for a lesser offence, or imposing inadequate compensation. 'Victim' under S. 2(wa) CrPC includes the legal heirs of the deceased. The victim's appeal under the proviso lies directly to the High Court without leave; this provision is to be liberally construed.
subsequent_treatment: Followed; survives BNSS transition (S. 413 carries forward).

title: Manu Sharma v. State (NCT of Delhi)
bench: P. Sathasivam, Swatanter Kumar, JJ.
facts: Murder of Jessica Lal; high-profile case based largely on circumstantial evidence.
issues: Was the conviction sustainable on the available circumstantial evidence?
Effect of media reporting on trial.
holding: Conviction sustained on chain of circumstantial evidence including ballistic evidence, witness testimony, recovery of weapon, and conduct of accused. Court observed that media trial cannot influence judicial decision-making but balanced reporting is permissible. Reaffirms Sharad Sarda principles for circumstantial evidence.
subsequent_treatment: Reference point for high-profile circumstantial evidence cases.

title: Shafhi Mohammad v. State of Himachal Pradesh
bench: Adarsh Kumar Goel, Uday Umesh Lalit, JJ.
bns_mapping: Evidence Act S. 65-B → BSA S. 63 (electronic evidence)
facts: Question whether S. 65-B certificate is mandatory for admitting electronic evidence in all cases.
issues: Is the requirement of S. 65-B certificate mandatory or directory?
holding: (1) The procedural requirement of certificate under S. 65-B(4) is not mandatory in all cases; the court may relax it where party producing evidence is not in possession of the device. The position in Anvar P.V. v. P.K. Basheer is clarified — the rigour applies primarily to private litigants who have access to devices. Note: This was overruled by the 3-Judge Bench in Arjun Panditrao Khotkar v. Kailash Kushanrao Gorantyal (2020) 7 SCC 1 — the certificate IS mandatory.
subsequent_treatment: Overruled by Arjun Panditrao Khotkar (2020) — current law: S. 65-B certificate is mandatory. Now governed by S. 63 BSA which carries forward the certificate requirement.

title: Arjun Panditrao Khotkar v. Kailash Kushanrao Gorantyal
bench: R. F. Nariman, S. Ravindra Bhat, V. Ramasubramanian, JJ.
facts: Reference to clarify the conflict between Anvar P.V. (2014) and Shafhi Mohammad (2018).
issues: Is the certificate under S. 65-B(4) Evidence Act mandatory for admitting electronic evidence?
holding: The certificate under S. 65-B(4) is mandatory for admitting secondary electronic evidence. Shafhi Mohammad v. State of H.P. (2018) overruled. Anvar P.V. v. P.K. Basheer (2014) affirmed. The certificate must be produced at the time of tendering the evidence; if unavailable, the party must apply to the appropriate authority for it. The original device itself is the only exception — primary evidence does not require S. 65-B certificate.
subsequent_treatment: Currently the leading authority; principles continue under BSA S. 63.

title: Vinubhai Haribhai Malaviya v. State of Gujarat
bench: R. F. Nariman, Surya Kant, V. Ramasubramanian, JJ.
statutes: Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — S. 156(3), S. 173(8)
facts: Magistrate's powers to direct further investigation after taking cognizance.
issues: Does the Magistrate have power to direct further investigation under S. 173(8) CrPC after taking cognizance?
holding: The Magistrate has the power to order further investigation under S. 173(8) CrPC even after cognizance has been taken, provided cognizance has not converted to commitment to Sessions. The Magistrate's power under S. 156(3) is broad and continues until commitment. Earlier conflicting view in Reeta Nag v. State of W.B. (2009) overruled. The need for fair investigation is paramount; the Magistrate is the watchdog of fair investigation.
subsequent_treatment: Leading authority on Magistrate's investigative oversight; survives BNSS transition.

title: Vihaan Kumar v. State of Haryana
bench: Abhay S. Oka, N. Kotiswar Singh, JJ.
facts: Petitioner arrested under various offences including S. 409 IPC; grounds of arrest were not communicated in writing.
issues: Does the Pankaj Bansal/Prabir Purkayastha rule of written grounds of arrest extend to all arrests under CrPC/BNSS?
holding: The constitutional requirement of furnishing grounds of arrest in writing under Article 22(1) is universal and applies to all arrests, including those under ordinary criminal law (CrPC/BNSS). The rule is not limited to special statutes like UAPA or PMLA. Any arrest without written grounds is unconstitutional and the arrestee is entitled to release. Article 22(1) admits no exception. Brings together the Pankaj Bansal and Prabir Purkayastha lines of authority.
subsequent_treatment: Foundational; standard practice for criminal courts post-2025.

title: Rajesh & Anr. v. State of Madhya Pradesh
bench: B. R. Gavai, J. B. Pardiwala, Sanjay Kumar, JJ.
facts: 15-year-old boy Ajit Pal was kidnapped and brutally murdered in March 2013. Trial court (Jabalpur) convicted three accused — Om Prakash Yadav under S. 364-A r/w S. 120-B IPC and his brother and son under S. 302 r/w S. 120-B IPC. Death sentence imposed on Raja Yadav and Rajesh Yadav. MP High Court confirmed conviction and death penalty. Prosecution case rested entirely on circumstantial evidence — last seen, recovery of mobiles, DNA from hair allegedly pulled by deceased, ransom call records.
issues: Whether the chain of circumstantial evidence was complete and consistent only with guilt?
Whether DNA evidence and recovery were credibly established?
Whether the case qualified as 'rarest of rare' for death penalty?
holding: Conviction and death sentence set aside; all three accused acquitted on benefit of doubt. The circumstantial chain was riddled with infirmities: (a) DNA evidence (hair allegedly pulled by deceased) inherently improbable given relative heights of accused and deceased and possibility of police plant; (b) link between Om Prakash Yadav and the mobile number from which ransom calls were made was not established by any document; (c) seizure of mobile phones from Om Prakash Yadav's house only on a second search after first search yielded nothing — unexplained; (d) call records of ransom number traced to one Bhuraji whom police never examined; (e) PW-2 introduced new facts in cross-examination not reflected in earlier statements. Court applied Sharad Birdhichand Sarda v. State of Maharashtra (1984) 4 SCC 116 — five golden principles for circumstantial evidence — and held them not satisfied. Court strongly criticised standards of police investigation and recommended a 'consistent and dependable code of investigation'. Reliance on Manoj v. State of Madhya Pradesh (2023) 2 SCC 353 on suspect DNA recovery.
subsequent_treatment: Recent leading authority on circumstantial-evidence acquittals where investigation is faulty. Strongly worded indictment of police investigation standards. Cited frequently in 2024-25 acquittal appeals.

title: Karuppudayar v. State Rep. by the Deputy Superintendent of Police, Lalgudi Trichy
bench: B. R. Gavai, Augustine George Masih, JJ.
statutes: Scheduled Castes and the Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act, 1989 — S. 3(1)(r), S. 3(1)(s)
bns_mapping: CrPC S. 482 → BNSS S. 528 (inherent powers retained)
facts: Appellant approached Revenue Inspector (Respondent No. 3, a member of Scheduled Caste) at the Revenue Divisional Office regarding inclusion of his father's name in the patta. A quarrel ensued during which the appellant allegedly abused the Revenue Inspector using his caste name inside the office. FIR was registered under Sections 294(b) and 353 IPC read with Sections 3(1)(r) and 3(1)(s) of the SC-ST Act. Appellant moved the Madras High Court under S. 482 CrPC for quashing; High Court dismissed. Hence the appeal.
issues: Whether the alleged caste-based abuse inside a government office, with no third party present, satisfies the 'in any place within public view' requirement under S. 3(1)(r) and 3(1)(s) of the SC-ST Act?
Whether the FIR and chargesheet should be quashed under S. 482 CrPC?
holding: Appeals allowed; FIR, chargesheet, and all consequential proceedings under S. Spl.S.C. No. 7 of 2022 quashed. The expression 'in any place within public view' under S. 3(1)(r) and 3(1)(s) of the SC-ST Act requires that the offence be committed in a place where it can be witnessed by an independent third party (member of the public) other than the accused and the complainant. Mere occurrence inside a public office (here, the Revenue Divisional Office) is insufficient if no independent witness was present. Reliance on Hitesh Verma v. State of Uttarakhand (2020) 10 SCC 710 and Priti Agarwalla v. State of GNCTD (2022) — both reaffirming the public-view test. Case falls within first category of Bhajan Lal — allegations even taken at face value do not constitute the alleged offence. High Court erred in not considering the public-view contention.
subsequent_treatment: Recent (2025) authority on the 'public view' requirement under SC-ST Act. Reaffirms Hitesh Verma v. State of Uttarakhand. Strong precedent for S. 482 / S. 528 BNSS quashing of SC-ST Act cases where alleged abuse is inside a closed office or private space.
